heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
paragraph_or_text_span: NICE guideline Reference number: NG20 Published: 02 September 2015 Download guidance (PDF)

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
paragraph_or_text_span: 1.1 Recognition of coeliac disease 1.2 Serological testing for coeliac disease 1.3 Referral of people with suspected coeliac disease 1.4 Monitoring in people with coeliac disease 1.5 Non‑responsive and refractory coeliac disease 1.6 Information and support 1.7 Advice on dietary management Terms used in this guideline

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
paragraph_or_text_span: People have the right to be involved in discussions and make informed decisions about their care, as described in NICE's information on making decisions about your care . Making decisions using NICE guidelines explains how we use words to show the strength (or certainty) of our recommendations, and has information about prescribing medicines (including off-label use), professional guidelines, stan

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer serological testing for coeliac disease to: people with any of the following: persistent unexplained abdominal or gastrointestinal symptoms faltering growth prolonged fatigue unexpected weight loss severe or persistent mouth ulcers unexplained iron, vitamin B12 or folate deficiency type 1 diabetes, at diagnosis autoimmune thyroid disease, at diagnosis irritable bowel syndrome (in adults) fir

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Consider serological testing for coeliac disease in people with any of the following: metabolic bone disorder (reduced bone mineral density or osteomalacia) unexplained neurological symptoms (particularly peripheral neuropathy or ataxia) unexplained subfertility or recurrent miscarriage persistently raised liver enzymes with unknown cause dental enamel defects Down's syndrome Turner syndrome.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
paragraph_or_text_span: For people undergoing investigations for coeliac disease: explain that any test is accurate only if a gluten‑containing diet is eaten during the diagnostic process and advise the person not to start a gluten‑free diet until diagnosis is confirmed by a specialist, even if the results of a serological test are positive.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Advise people who are following a normal diet (containing gluten) to eat some gluten in more than 1 meal every day for at least 6 weeks before testing.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
paragraph_or_text_span: If people who have restricted their gluten intake or excluded gluten from their diet are reluctant or unable to re‑introduce gluten into their diet before testing: refer the person to a gastrointestinal specialist and explain that it may be difficult to confirm their diagnosis by intestinal biopsy.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Advise people who have tested negative for coeliac disease, particularly first‑degree relatives and people with type 1 diabetes, that: coeliac disease may present with a wide range of symptoms and they should consult their healthcare professional if any of the symptoms listed in recommendations 1.1.1 or 1.1.2 arise or persist.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Do not offer serological testing for coeliac disease in infants before gluten has been introduced into the diet.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
paragraph_or_text_span: All serological tests should be undertaken in laboratories with clinical pathology accreditation (CPA) or ISO15189 accreditation.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
paragraph_or_text_span: When healthcare professionals request serological tests to investigate suspected coeliac disease in young people and adults, laboratories should: test for total immunoglobulin A (IgA) and IgA tissue transglutaminase (tTG) as the first choice use IgA endomysial antibodies (EMA) if IgA tTG is weakly positive consider using IgG EMA, IgG deamidated gliadin peptide (DGP) or IgG tTG if IgA is deficient

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
paragraph_or_text_span: When healthcare professionals request serological tests to investigate suspected coeliac disease in children, laboratories should: test for total IgA and IgA tTG as the first choice consider using IgG EMA, IgG DGP or IgG tTG if IgA is deficient.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
paragraph_or_text_span: When laboratories test for total IgA, a specific assay designed to measure total IgA levels should be used.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Do not use human leukocyte antigen (HLA) DQ2 (DQ2.2 and DQ2.5)/DQ8 testing in the initial diagnosis of coeliac disease in non‑specialist settings.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Only consider using HLA DQ2 (DQ2.2 and DQ2.5)/DQ8 testing in the diagnosis of coeliac disease in specialist settings (for example, in children who are not having a biopsy, or in people who already have limited gluten ingestion and choose not to have a gluten challenge).

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Laboratories should clearly communicate the interpretation of serological test results and recommended action to healthcare professionals.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
1.3 Referral of people with suspected coeliac disease
paragraph_or_text_span: Refer young people and adults with positive serological test results to a gastrointestinal specialist for endoscopic intestinal biopsy to confirm or exclude coeliac disease. [In young people and adults, a positive serological test result is defined as unambiguously positive IgA tTG alone, or weakly positive IgA tTG and a positive IgA EMA test result. Note: In people who have IgA deficiency, a sero

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
1.3 Referral of people with suspected coeliac disease
paragraph_or_text_span: Refer children with positive serological test results to a paediatric gastroenterologist or paediatrician with a specialist interest in gastroenterology for further investigation for coeliac disease. [Further investigation may include, but is not limited to, one or more of the following: an IgA EMA test to confirm serological positivity, HLA genetic testing, an endoscopic biopsy].

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
1.3 Referral of people with suspected coeliac disease
paragraph_or_text_span: Refer people with negative serological test results to a gastrointestinal specialist for further assessment if coeliac disease is still clinically suspected.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
1.3 Referral of people with suspected coeliac disease
paragraph_or_text_span: Healthcare professionals should have a low threshold for re‑testing people identified in recommendations 1.1.1 or 1.1.2 if they develop any symptoms consistent with coeliac disease.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
1.4 Monitoring in people with coeliac disease
paragraph_or_text_span: Consider referring people with coeliac disease for endoscopic intestinal biopsy if continued exposure to gluten has been excluded and: serological titres are persistently high and show little or no change after 12 months or they have persistent symptoms, including diarrhoea, abdominal pain, weight loss, fatigue or unexplained anaemia.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
1.4 Monitoring in people with coeliac disease
paragraph_or_text_span: Do not use serological testing alone to determine whether gluten has been excluded from the person's diet.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
1.4 Monitoring in people with coeliac disease
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer an annual review to people with coeliac disease. During the review: measure weight and height review symptoms consider the need for assessment of diet and adherence to the gluten‑free diet consider the need for specialist dietetic and nutritional advice.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
1.4 Monitoring in people with coeliac disease
paragraph_or_text_span: Refer the person to a GP or consultant if concerns are raised in the annual review. The GP or consultant should assess all of the following: the need for a dual‑energy X‑ray absorptiometry (DEXA) scan (in line with the NICE guideline on osteoporosis: assessing the risk of fragility fracture ) or active treatment of bone disease the need for specific blood tests the risk of long‑term complications

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Consider the following actions in people with coeliac disease who have persistent symptoms despite advice to exclude gluten from their diet: review the certainty of the original diagnosis refer the person to a specialist dietitian to investigate continued exposure to gluten investigate potential complications or coexisting conditions that may be causing persistent symptoms, such as irritable bowel

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Diagnose refractory coeliac disease if the original diagnosis of coeliac disease has been confirmed, and exposure to gluten and any coexisting conditions have been excluded as the cause of continuing symptoms.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Refer people with refractory coeliac disease to a specialist centre for further investigation.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Consider prednisolone for the initial management of the symptoms of refractory coeliac disease in adults while waiting for specialist advice. See recommendations on managing glucocorticoid withdrawal in NICE's guideline on adrenal insufficiency .

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Explain to people who are thought to be at risk of coeliac disease that a delayed diagnosis, or undiagnosed coeliac disease, can result in continuing ill health and serious long‑term complications.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Give people with coeliac disease (and their family members or carers, where appropriate) sources of information on the disease, including national and local specialist coeliac groups and dietitians with a specialist knowledge in coeliac disease.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
paragraph_or_text_span: A healthcare professional with a specialist knowledge of coeliac disease should tell people with a confirmed diagnosis of coeliac disease (and their family members or carers, where appropriate) about the importance of a gluten‑free diet and give them information to help them follow it. This should include: information on which types of food contain gluten and suitable alternatives, including glute

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Be aware that people with coeliac disease may experience anxiety and depression. Diagnose and manage these issues in line with the following NICE guidelines: Depression in adults with a chronic physical health problem Depression in children and young people Generalised anxiety disorder and panic disorder (with or without agoraphobia) in adults Social anxiety disorder .

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Explain to people with coeliac disease and their family members or carers (as appropriate) that the Department of Health's Green Book recommends immunisation against pneumococcus.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Advise people with coeliac disease (and their family members or carers, where appropriate) to seek advice from a member of their healthcare team if they are thinking about taking over‑the‑counter vitamin or mineral supplements.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Explain to people with coeliac disease (and their family members or carers, where appropriate) that they may need to take specific supplements such as calcium or vitamin D if their dietary intake is insufficient.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Explain to people with coeliac disease (and their family members or carers, where appropriate) that: they can choose to include gluten‑free oats in their diet at any stage and they will be advised whether to continue eating gluten‑free oats depending on their immunological, clinical or histological response.

heading_path: Coeliac disease: recognition, assessment and management
paragraph_or_text_span: IgA deficiency is defined as total IgA less than 0.07 g per litre.
